title: Off-Hours VPN Access
dataSources: VPN session logs, HR data (department, role)
spl:
index=vpn sourcetype="cisco:asa" action="session_connect"
| eval hour=tonumber(strftime(_time,"%H"))
| where (hour < 5 OR hour >= 22)
| lookup user_roles.csv user OUTPUT department, role
| where role!="on_call" AND role!="sysadmin"
| table _time, user, department, src, hour
description: VPN access at unusual hours may indicate compromised credentials or unauthorized activity. Alerting supports investigation.
value: Security teams baseline normal VPN usage patterns and flag off-hours connections from non-shift employees, adding a behavioral context layer that strengthens anomaly detection when combined with geo and auth indicators.
implementation: Define normal hours per user role/department. Alert on VPN connections outside hours for roles that don't require it. Whitelist on-call and sysadmin roles. Review weekly for patterns.
visualization: Heatmap (user × hour of day), Table (off-hours access), Bar chart (off-hours by department).
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic
  by All_Traffic.user, All_Traffic.action, All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest span=1h
| sort - count
knownFalsePositives: Anomalies from new SaaS app rollouts, browser updates, or vendor URL category changes.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Splunk Add-on for Cisco ASA (`sourcetype=cisco:asa`). Data in `index=vpn`.
• Key fields: `user`, `src`, `action=session_connect`, `_time`.
• Create a `user_roles.csv` lookup: `user,department,role,work_schedule` (e.g., work_schedule='9-18' or 'shift'). Users with `shift` schedules should not trigger off-hours alerts.
• Define off-hours per your organization. Typical: weekdays before 6am or after 10pm, and all day weekends.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Verify connection timestamps span all hours:
```spl
index=vpn sourcetype="cisco:asa" action="session_connect" earliest=-7d
| eval hour=tonumber(strftime(_time, "%H"))
| chart count over hour
```
This should show a typical workday pattern with peaks at 8-9am. If all connections are concentrated in business hours, off-hours monitoring has limited value.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert

Primary search — Off-hours VPN access:
```spl
index=vpn sourcetype="cisco:asa" action="session_connect" earliest=-24h
| eval hour=tonumber(strftime(_time, "%H"))
| eval day_of_week=strftime(_time, "%u")
| eval off_hours=if((hour<6 OR hour>=22) OR day_of_week>5, 1, 0)
| where off_hours=1
| lookup user_roles.csv user OUTPUT department role work_schedule
| where work_schedule!="shift" OR isnull(work_schedule)
| iplocation src
| stats count as sessions values(Country) as countries latest(_time) as last_access by user, department, role
| sort - sessions
```

Understanding this SPL: Identifies non-shift employees connecting outside business hours, excluding known shift workers. Off-hours connections are not inherently malicious — many employees work late — but they are statistically unusual and increase risk. Combined with geo-anomalies (UC-17.2.3) or auth failures (UC-17.2.2), off-hours access becomes a stronger indicator of compromise.

Step 3 — Validate
(a) Check with a known after-hours worker — their connections should appear.
(b) Verify time zone handling: Splunk uses the search head's timezone by default. If users are in multiple timezones, adjust with `| eval local_hour=...`.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard: Row 1 — "Off-hours connections (24h)", "Unique users". Row 2 — Heatmap: connections by hour and day. Row 3 — Table: off-hours users.
Alert: Daily digest of off-hours connections. Immediate for off-hours + geo-anomaly combination.

Runbook:
1. **Known late worker** — Baseline and exclude from future alerts.
2. **Unexpected off-hours + unusual country** — High-risk. Verify with the user immediately.
3. **Off-hours surge (many users)** — Incident response scenario (e.g., production outage requiring many engineers). Document.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting
• **Timezone confusion** — Ensure the ASA sends timestamps in UTC and Splunk converts correctly.
• **All connections appear off-hours** — The ASA clock may be wrong. Verify with `show clock` on the ASA.
grandmaExplanation: We keep an eye on who connects to our network very late at night or on weekends when they normally would not, which could mean someone else is using their credentials.
hardware: Cisco ASA 5506-X/5508-X/5516-X/5525-X/5545-X/5555-X, ASAv, Cisco Secure Firewall 3100/4200; Palo Alto PA-220/PA-440/PA-3200/PA-5200, GlobalProtect; Fortinet FortiGate 60F/100F/200F/600F/1800F, FortiGate SSL-VPN; Juniper SRX300/SRX1500/SRX4100/SRX4200, Junos Dynamic VPN
